title: 第 15 節
en: The Persian translator seems to have confined himself to the second Arawyaka 2, to which he gives various titles, Sarbsar, Asarbeh, Antrteheh. That Antrteheh si^l is a misreading of *±/u) was pointed out long ago by Burnouf, and the same explanation applies probably to ju^A asarbeh, and if to that, then to Sarbsar also. No explanation has ever been given why the Aitareya-upanishad should have been called Sarvasara, which Professor Weber thinks was corrupted into Sarbsar. At all events the Aitareyaupanishad is not the Sarvasara-upanishad, the Oupnek'hat Sarb, more correctly called Sarvopanishatsara, and ascribed either to the Taittiriyaka or to the Atharva-veda 3.

The Aitareya-upanishad, properly so called, has been edited and translated in the Bibliotheca Indica by Dr. Roer. The whole of the Aitareya-arawyaka with Saya/za's commentary was published in the same series by Rajendralal Mitra.

Though I have had several MSS. of the text and commentary at my disposal, I have derived little aid from them, but have throughout endeavoured to restore that text which .Sahkara (the pupil of Govinda) and Sayawa had before them. Saya;/a, for the Upanishad portion, follows 5ahkara's commentary, of which we have a gloss by A.
Ananda^nana.

Colebrooke in his Essays (vol. ii, p. 42) says that he 1 This may have been the origin of a Rishi Mahaitareya, by the side of the Rishi Aitareya, mentioned in the Asvalayana GWhya-sutras III, 4 (ed. Stenzler). Professor Weber takes Aitareya and Mahaitareya here as names of works, but he admits that in the iSankhayana Grihya-sutras they are clearly names of Rishis (Ind. Stud. I, p. 3S9).
2 He translates II, 1— II, 3, 4, leaving out the rest of the third adhyaya; afterwards II, 4-II, J.
3 Bibliotheca Indica, the Atharva«a-upanishads, p. 394.

possessed one gloss by Naraya/zendra on 5ankara's commentary, and another by Anandatirtha on a different gloss for the entire Upanishad. The gloss by Narayaz/endra 1, however, is, so Dr. Rost informs me, the same as that of Ananda^fiana, while, so far as I can see, the gloss contained in MS. E. I. H. 2386 (also MS. Wilson 401), to which Colebrooke refers, is not a gloss by Anandatirtha at all, but a gloss by Vijve^varatirtha on a commentary by AnandatirthabhagavatpadaMrya, also called Pun/apra^na/'arya, who explained the whole of the Mahaitareya-upanishad from a Vaisb//ava point of view.
THE KAUSHITAKI-BRAHMAjVA-UPANISHAD.

The Kaushitaki-upanishad, or, as it is more properly called, the Kaushitaki-brahma^a-upanishad, belongs, like the Aitareya-upanishad, to the followers of the Rig-veda. It was translated into Persian under the title of Kokhenk, and has been published in the Bibliotheca Indica with vSarikarananda's commentary and an excellent translation by Professor Cowell.

Though it is called the Kaushitaki-brahma/za-upanishad, it does not form part of the Kaushitaki-brahmawa in 30 adhyayas which we possess, and we must therefore account for its name by admitting that the Arawyaka, of which it formed a portion, could be reckoned as part of the Brahma;/a literature of the Rig-veda (see Aitareya-ara/zyaka, Introduction, p. xcii), and that hence the Upanishad might be called the Upanishad of the Brahma^a of the Kaushitakins 2.

From a commentary discovered by Professor Cowell it appears that the four adhyayas of this Upanishad 1 A MS. in the Notices of Sanskrit MSS., vol. ii, p. 133, ascribed to Abhinavanarayarcendra, called Atmasha/kabhashyatfka, begins like the gloss edited by Dr. Roer, and ends like Sayarca's commentary on the seventh adhyaya, as edited by Rajendralal Mitra. The same name is given in MS. Wilson 94, ^rhnatkaivalyendrasarasvatipu^yapadasishya-srimadabhinavanaraya^endrasarasvati.
'J A Maha-kaushitaki-brahmawa is quoted, but has not yet been met with.

INTRODUCTION. XC1X were followed by five other adhyayas, answering, so far as we can judge from a few extracts, to some of the adhyayas of the Aitareya-arawyaka, while an imperfect MS. of an A Ara;/yaka in the Royal Library at Berlin (Weber, Catalogue, p. 20) begins, like the Aitareya-ara;/yaka, with a description of the Mahavrata, followed by discussions on the uktha in the second adhyaya ; and then proceeds in the third adhyaya to give the story of A"itra Garigyayani in the same words as the Kaushitaki-upanishad in the first adhyaya. Other MSS. again adopt different divisions. In one MS. of the commentary (MS. A), the four adhyayas of the Upanishad are counted as sixth, seventh, eighth, and ninth (ending with ityara/zyake navamo 'dhyaya//) ; in another (MS. P) the third and fourth adhyayas of the Upanishad are quoted as the fifth and sixth of the Kaushitakyarawyaka, possibly agreeing therefore, to a certain extent, with the Berlin MS. In a MS. of the .Sarikhayana Arawyaka in the Royal Library at Berlin, there are 15 adhyayas, 1 and 2 A • • - corresponding to Ait. Ar. 1 and 5; 3-6 containing the Kau- A shitaki-upanishad ; 7 and 8 corresponding to Ait. Ar. 3 *. Poley seems to have known a MS. in which the four adhyayas of the Upanishad formed the first, seventh, eighth, and ninth adhyayas of a Kaushitaki-brahmawa.

As there were various recensions of the Kaushitaki-brahma«a (the vSankhayana, Kauthuma, &c), the Upanishad also exists in at least two texts. The commentator, in some of its MSS., refers to the various readings of the vSakhas, explaining them, whenever there seems to be occasion for it. I have generally followed the text which is presupposed by ^Sahkarananda's Dipika, and contained in MSS. F, G (Cowell, Preface, p. v), so far as regards the third and fourth adhyayas. According to Professor Cowell, Vidyarawya in his Sarvopanishadarthanubh.utipraka.ra followed the text of the commentary, while ^Saiikara^arya, if we may trust to extracts in his commentary on the Vedanta-sutras, followed the other text, contained in MS. A (Cowell, Preface, p. v).
1 See Weber, History of Sanskrit Literature, p. 50.

The style of the commentator differs in so marked a manner from that of .Saiikara^arya, that even without the fact that the author of the commentary on the Kaushitakiupanishad is called vSankarananda, it would have been difficult to ascribe it, as has been done by some scholars, to the famous vSankaraMrya. 6arikarananda is called the teacher of Madhava^arya (Hall, Index, p. 98), and the disciple of Anandatma Muni (Hall, Index, p. 116).

I have had the great advantage of being able to consult for the Kaushitaki-upanishad, not only the text and commentary as edited by Professor Cowell, but also his excellent translation. If I differ from him in some points, this is but natural, considering the character of the text and the many difficulties that have still to be solved, before we can hope to arrive at a full understanding of these ancient philosophical treatises.
the vAgasaneyi-saj/hitA-upanishad.

The Va^asaneyi-sawhita-upanishad, commonly called from its beginning, Isa. or Lavasya, forms the fortieth and concluding chapter of the Sawhita of the White Ya^-ur-veda. If the Sawhitas are presupposed by the Brahmawas, at least in that form in which we possess them, then this Upanishad, being the only one that forms part of a Sawhita, might claim a very early age. The Sawhita of the White Ya^-ur-veda, however, is acknowledged to be of modern origin, as compared with the Sawhita of the Black Ya^-ur-veda, and it would not be safe therefore to ascribe to this Upanishad a much higher antiquity than to those which have found a place in the older Brahma;/as and Arawyakas.
There are differences between the text, as contained in the Ya^-ur-veda-sa;«hita, and the text of the Upanishad by itself. Those which are of some interest have been mentioned in the notes.

In some notes appended to the translation of this Upanishad I have called attention to what seems to me INTRODUCTION. CI its peculiar character, namely, the recognition of the necessity of works as a preparation for the reception of the highest knowledge. This agrees well with the position occupied by this Upanishad at the end of the Sa;;/hita, in which the sacrificial works and the hymns that are to accompany them are contained. The doctrine that the moment a man is enlightened, he becomes free, as taught in other Upanishads, led to a rejection of all discipline and a condemnation of all sacrifices, which could hardly have been tolerated in the last chapter of the Ya^ur-vedasawhita, the liturgical Veda par excellence.

Other peculiarities of this Upanishad are the name Is, lord, a far more personal name for the highest Being than Brahman ; the asurya (demoniacal) or asurya (sunless) worlds to which all go who have lost their self; Mataruvan, used in the sense of prawa or spirit ; asnaviram, without muscles, in the sense of incorporeal ; and the distinction between sambhuti and asambhuti in verses 12-14.
The editions of the text, commentaries, and glosses, and the earlier translations may be seen in the works quoted before, p. lxxxiv.
First Khajvda1.
i. Let a man meditate on the syllable2 Om, called the udgltha ; for the udgitha (a portion of the Sama-veda) is sung, beginning with Om.

The full account, however, of Om is this : — 2. The essence 3 of all beings is the earth, the essence of the earth is water, the essence of water 1 The A^andogya-upanishad begins with recommending meditation on the syllable Om, a sacred syllable that had to be pronounced at the beginning of each Veda and of every recitation of Vedic hymns. As connected with the Sama-veda, that syllable Om is called udgitha. Its more usual name is prawava. The object of the Upanishad is to explain the various meanings which the syllable Om may assume in the mind of a devotee, some of them being extremely artificial and senseless, till at last the highest meaning of Om is reached, viz. Brahman, the intelligent cause of the universe.
2 Akshara means both syllable and the imperishable, i.e. Brahman.

3 Essence, rasa, is explained in different ways, as origin, support, end, cause, and effect. Rasa means originally the sap of trees. That sap may be conceived either as the essence extracted from the tree, or as what gives vigour and life to a tree. In the former case it might be transferred to the conception of effect, in the latter to that of cause. In our sentence it has sometimes the one, sometimes the other meaning. Earth is the support of all beings, water pervades the earth, plants arise from water, man lives by plants, speech is the best part of man, the Rig-veda the best part of speech, the Sama-veda the best extract from the Ri'k, udgitha, or the syllable Om, the crown of the Sama-veda.
I' [3] B A'tfANDOGYA-UPANISHAD.
zh: 波斯譯者似乎只侷限於第二部阿蘭尼亞克（Arawyaka）2，並給予它各種標題，即沙爾薩爾（Sarbsar）、阿薩爾貝（Asarbeh）、安特爾特赫（Antrteheh）。布爾努夫（Burnouf）早已指出安特爾特赫（Antrteheh）是※u)的誤讀，同樣的解釋可能也適用於朱※阿（ju^A）阿薩爾貝，若適用於此，則亦適用於沙爾薩爾。至於為何愛多雷亞奧義書（Aitareya-upanishad）應被稱為薩爾瓦薩拉（Sarvasara），一直未曾有過解釋；韋伯教授認為此名被訛誤為沙爾薩爾（Sarbsar）。無論如何，愛多雷亞奧義書並非薩爾瓦薩拉奧義書（Sarvasara-upanishad），亦非烏佩涅克哈特薩爾布（Oupnek'hat Sarb），更確切地稱為薩爾沃帕尼沙薩拉（Sarvopanishatsara），並被歸於泰鐵利亞卡（Taittiriyaka）或雅吠陀（Atharva-veda）3。
《愛多羅耶奧義書》（Aitareya-upanishad）本身，已由羅爾博士（Dr. Roer）在《印度文庫叢書》（Bibliotheca Indica）中編輯並譯出。整部《愛多羅耶阿蘭耶卡》（Aitareya-arawyaka）及其沙亞那（Saya/za）的評註，亦由拉金德拉拉爾‧米特拉（Rajendralal Mitra）在同一叢書中出版。
雖然我掌握了多份文本與註疏的手稿，但從中獲益甚少，我始終致力於復原商羯羅（戈文達之弟子）與薩耶那所見之文本。在奧義書部分，薩耶那遵循商羯羅的註疏，我們擁有其中一份按語文獻。

I appreciate your detailed instructions, but I notice the text you've provided for translation appears to be only "Ananda^nana" — which seems to be a Sanskrit term (आनन्द, meaning "bliss" or "joy") rather than a passage from Müller's 1873 introduction. Could you please provide the complete English original text that you'd like me to translate? Once you share the full passage from Müller's work, I'll provide an accurate translation following all your specifications for academic rigor, traditional Chinese terminology, and proper rendering of the technical vocabulary in religious studies.
科爾布魯克在其《論文集》（第二卷，第42頁）中指出，他¹ 這可能是一位仙人摩訶愛達耶亞（Mahaitareya）的起源，他與梵經《阿斯瓦拉亞那住家經》第三卷第4節（史坦茲勒版本）所提及的仙人愛達耶亞（Aitareya）並列。韋伯教授在此處將愛達耶亞和摩訶愛達耶亞視為著作的名稱，但他承認在《商卡雅那住家經》中，它們顯然是仙人的名字（《印度研究》第一卷，第389頁）。
他翻譯了第二編第一至第二編第三、四章，省略了第三篇章的其餘部分；之後翻譯第二編第四至第二編第五。
3 《印度文庫》，《雅闥婆吠陀奧義書》(Atharvaveda-upanishads)，第394頁。

擁有一份那羅耶那（Naraya/zendra）針對商羯羅（5ankara）註疏所作的釋義，以及另一份阿難陀蒂爾塔（Anandatirtha）針對整部奧義書另一釋義所作的註解。那羅耶那的釋義，然而如羅斯特博士告知我者，與阿難陀吉亞那（Ananda^fiana）的釋義相同，而就我所能見者而言，羅斯特先生所指涉的手稿E. I. H. 2386（亦為手稿Wilson 401）所含之釋義，根本不是阿難陀蒂爾塔所作的釋義，而是毗耶婆羅蒂爾塔（Vijve^varatirtha）針對阿難陀蒂爾塔婆伽瓦帕達阿查里亞（AnandatirthabhagavatpadaMrya）所作之註疏的釋義，此人亦稱為布那普拉那阿查里亞（Pun/apra^na/'arya），他從毗濕奴派（Vaisb//ava）的觀點詮釋了整部馬哈埃塔耶亞奧義書（Mahaitareya-upanishad）。
《考希塔基梵書奧義書》(The Kaushitaki-Brahma-Upanishad)

《考希塔基奧義書》（Kaushitaki-upanishad），或者更恰當地說，《考希塔基梵天奧義書》（Kaushitaki-brahma­ṇa-upanishad），與《愛達羅耶奧義書》（Aitareya-upanishad）一樣，屬於梨俱吠陀的追隨者。它曾被譯成波斯文，題名為《科漢克》（Kokhenk），並已在《印度文庫叢書》（Bibliotheca Indica）中刊行，並附有商羯羅阿難陀的評釋及科威爾教授的優秀譯文。

雖然它被稱為《考希達基梵書奧義書》（Kaushitaki-brahma/za-upanishad），但它並不構成我們所擁有的三十章《考希達基梵書》（Kaushitaki-brahmawa）的一部分，因此我們必須通過承認該《森林書》（Arawyaka）曾是《梨俱吠陀》的《梵書》文獻的一部分，來解釋其名稱的由來（參見《艾達瑞亞森林書》導論，第九十二頁），進而該奧義書可被稱為《考希達基人的梵書奧義書》²。

根據考威爾教授所發現的一部註釋，此奧義書的四個 adhyaya 1 《梵文手稿通知》第二卷，第133頁的手稿，歸屬於阿比納瓦那羅亞仙德拉（Abhinavanarayarcendra）所著，稱為《我性釋義》（Atmasha/kabhashyatfka），其開篇與羅耶爾博士編訂的註疏相同，結尾則與薩亞納對第七 adhyaya 的註釋相同，後者由拉真德拉拉爾‧米特拉編訂。同一名稱亦見於威爾遜手稿94號，^rhnatkaivalyendrasarasvatipu^yapadasishya-srimadabhinavanaraya^endrasarasvati。
《大考希他基梵書》（Maha-kaushitaki-brahmawa）被引用過，但至今尚未被發現。

導論。XC1X 之後有五部其他的經章（adhyayas），據我們從若干摘錄所能判斷，相應於《艾達雷亞‧阿蘭亞卡》（Aitareya-arawyaka）的某些經章，而柏林皇家圖書館內一部不完整的《阿蘭亞卡》手稿（Weber, Catalogue, p. 20）開始部分與《艾達雷亞‧阿蘭亞卡》相同，以《摩訶瓦拉塔》（Mahavrata）的描述起首，其次在第二經章討論烏克塔（uktha），然後在第三經章敍述阿德里特拉‧迦利亞雅尼（A"itra Garigyayani）的故事，其措辭與《考希塔基‧奧義書》（Kaushitaki-upanishad）第一經章完全相同。其他手稿再採取不同的區分方式。在一部注疏手稿（MS. A）中，《奧義書》的四部經章被計為第六、七、八、九（以 ityara/zyake navamo 'dhyaya// 結尾）；在另一部（MS. P）中，《奧義書》的第三、四經章被引用為《考希塔基‧阿蘭亞卡》的第五、六經章，因而在某種程度上可能與柏林手稿相符。在柏林皇家圖書館的《尚卡亞那‧阿蘭亞卡》手稿中，共有十五部經章，其中第一、二部分別相應於《艾達雷亞‧阿蘭亞卡》第一、五經章；第三至六部包含《考希塔基‧奧義書》；第七、八部相應於《艾達雷亞‧阿蘭亞卡》第三經章*。波萊（Poley）似乎曾見過一部手稿，其中《奧義書》的四部經章構成《考希塔基‧婆羅門書》（Kaushitaki-brahmawa）的第一、七、八、九經章。

由於《考希塔基梵書》（Kaushitaki-brahma«a）存在各種版本（如婆叉耶那派、考圖摩派等），《考希塔基奧義書》（Kaushitaki-Upanishad）也至少存在兩種文本。在某些手稿中，注疏者參考各個毘舍迦派（vSakhas）的異文，並在認為有必要之處對其加以解釋。就第三和第四篇章而言，我通常遵循商卡拉南達的《光疏》（^Sahkarananda's Dipika）所預設、且包含在手稿F、G中的文本（Cowell《前言》，第v頁）。根據考威爾教授的說法，毘第亞樂尼亞在其《一切奧義書義疏詳釋》（Sarvopanishadarthanubh.utipraka.ra）中遵循了注疏文本，而商卡羅笈多（^Saiikara^arya）如果我們可以相信其《韋檀多經論》（Vedanta-sutras）注疏中的摘錄，則遵循了包含在手稿A中的另一種文本（Cowell《前言》，第v頁）。
見韋伯《梵文文獻史》，第50頁。

評論者的文體與商羯羅（vSankara）的風格差異極大，即便沒有《考希他基奧義書》（Kaushitakiupanishad）評論作者被稱為商羯羅難陀（vSankarananda）這一事實，某些學者試圖將其歸為著名的商羯羅（vSankara）也會顯得困難重重。商羯羅難陀被稱為摩德婆（Madhava）的導師（Hall, Index, p. 98），且是阿難達摩尼聖者（Anandatma Muni）的弟子（Hall, Index, p. 116）。
我有幸能夠查閱《考希陀基奧義書》（Kaushitaki-upanishad），不僅參考了考韋爾教授編訂的文本與註疏，還能取用他的精湛譯文。若我在某些要點上與他的見解有異，這乃是自然之事，考慮到文本本身的特質，以及在我們能夠期待充分理解這些古代哲學論著之前，仍有許多困難有待解決。
瓦亞薩內亞梵經奧義書（vAgasaneyi-saj/hitA-upanishad）。

《瓦婆舍尼吠陀奧義書》（Vāśāneyi-saṃhitā-upaniṣad），通常以其開篇詞稱為《伊沙奧義書》或《拉瓦舍婆奧義書》，構成白色雅柔吠陀（White Yajur-veda）的《本集》（Saṃhitā）的第四十章，亦即其最後一章。若《本集》為《婆羅門書》所預設，至少在我們所掌握的那種形式中確為如此，則此奧義書作為唯一構成《本集》一部分的奧義書，可能主張其年代極為久遠。然而，白色雅柔吠陀的《本集》與黑色雅柔吠陀（Black Yajur-veda）的《本集》相比，公認其起源較晚，因此將此奧義書的歷史年代定得遠早於那些見於較古老的《婆羅門書》與《阿蘭耶卡書》（Āraṇyaka）中的奧義書，乃為不妥之舉。
梵文文本在《夜柔吠陀》（Yajur-veda-saṃhitā）中的形式與《奧義書》本身的文本之間存在差異。那些具有相當興趣的差異已在註釋中提及。

在這部《奧義書》的譯文所附註中，我曾指出其特殊性質，在我看來這一點值得注意，即對於作為接受最高知識之準備的業行（works）之必要性的認可。這與該《奧義書》在《本集》（Samhitā）末尾所處的位置相符，該《本集》含有祭祀儀式及其所應伴隨的讚歌。而其他《奧義書》所教導的—人一旦開悟，便即刻得自由的學說—會導致對一切紀律的拒斥與對一切祭祀的譴責，這在作為禮儀吠陀至上代表的《夜柔吠陀本集》（Yajur-veda-samhitā）之最後一章中，幾乎是難以被容許的。

這部奧義書的其他特色包括：以「伊沙」（Is）稱呼主宰，這是比梵天（Brahman）更具人格性的最高存有之名稱；「阿蘇利亞」（asurya）即魔鬼般的或無日光的世界，所有喪失自我者均往生於此；「摩多魯瓦」（Mataruvan）用以表示「普拉瓦」（prawa）即精神之意；「阿斯那毗羅」（asnaviram）意為無肌肉，即非物質性；以及第十二至十四詩節中「三昧不提」（sambhuti）與「阿三昧不提」（asambhuti）之間的區分。
文本的版本、評註和詞彙表，以及較早的譯本可見於前述引用的著作，第 lxxxiv 頁。
¹ 梵文作 *Rig-Veda*。
i. 讓一個人冥想音節² Om，稱為 udgltha；因為 udgitha（《沙摩吠陀》的一部分）的吟誦是以 Om 開始的。

關於唵（Om）的完整說明如下：——2. 一切眾生的本質是大地，大地的本質是水，水的本質是 --- **註：** 原文在此處中斷。按文脈，應繼續為《昌多迦奧義書》(Chandogya-upanishad) 的內容。以下為原文完整的前置說明： 《昌多迦奧義書》以冥想唵（Om）這一神聖音節開篇，此音節須在每部吠陀及每次吠陀讚歌的誦讀開始時發音。作為與娑摩吠陀（Sama-veda）相關的音節，唵稱為「鄔得吉他」(udgitha)。其更常用的名稱是「普拉瓦」(prawava)。該奧義書的目的是闡明唵這一音節在虔信者心中可能承載的各種含義——其中某些含義極為人為造作、毫無意義——直至最終達到唵的最高意義，即梵（Brahman），宇宙的智慧因由。
2 Akshara（阿剎羅）兼具音節與不可摧毀之義，即梵天（Brahman）。

3 本質（rasa）有多種解釋方式，可指源起、支持、終極、原因與結果。Rasa 最初之義為樹液。該樹液可被理解為從樹中提取的精華，或者是賦予樹活力與生命的物質。前者可轉移至結果的概念，後者則轉移至原因的概念。在我們的論述中，它有時具有前者之義，有時具有後者之義。地是所有生命的支持，水遍布大地，植物生於水，人依植物而生，言語是人類最殊勝的部分，梨俱吠陀（Rig-veda）是言語中最殊勝的部分，沙摩吠陀（Sama-veda）是梨俱吠陀最精妙的提煉，烏吒歌（udgitha）或音節嗡（Om）乃沙摩吠陀的冠冕。
